runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity with respect to non-labor income for prime-age employed workers in the United States, holding net-of-tax wage constant and excluding participation responses.
citations: Killingsworth & Heckman (1986) handbook chapter on labor supply
Pencavel (1986) empirical studies of labor supply
MaCurdy (1981) intertemporal substitution in labor supply
runs.reasoningSummary: The income elasticity of labor supply (intensive margin) is generally estimated to be small and negative for prime-age workers. The substitution effect (higher income encourages work) is typically outweighed by the income effect (higher income allows less work), yielding net negative elasticities around -0.05 to -0.15. My point estimate of -0.10 reflects the consensus from labor supply literature that non-labor income induces modest reductions in hours worked. The range reflects uncertainty about heterogeneity across demographics and time periods.

The substitution effect (higher income encourages work) is typically outweighed by the income effect (higher income allows less work), yielding net negative elasticities around -0.05 to -0.15. runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity of labor supply with respect to non-labor income for prime-age workers in the US who are already employed, holding the net-of-tax wage constant.

runs.reasoningSummary: The income elasticity of labor supply on the intensive margin is typically small and negative for prime-age workers. Economic theory predicts a negative relationship (income effect): higher non-labor income reduces desired work hours. Empirical estimates from quasi-experimental and structural methods generally find elasticities in the range of -0.05 to -0.20, with considerable variation across populations and study designs. For the specific population (already-employed prime-age workers), income effects are typically modest. My point estimate centers around -0.10, reflecting the consensus that effects are present but small in magnitude, with a distribution allowing substantial uncertainty.

Economic theory predicts a negative relationship (income effect): higher non-labor income reduces desired work hours. Empirical estimates from quasi-experimental and structural methods generally find elasticities in the range of -0.05 to -0.20, with considerable variation across populations and study designs. runs.reasoningSummary: The income effect on labor supply for employed prime-age workers is typically small and negative: higher non-labor income tends to reduce hours worked modestly as workers substitute leisure for consumption. The wide quantile range reflects genuine uncertainty in this literature.

runs.reasoningSummary: Income effects on labor supply are typically negative and modest for prime-age workers.
